Design system  /  Components  /  Integrations block
IntegrationsBlock · explainer with FAQ + product-mock
Integrations

Apps that truly work together.

OpenRegister is what turns an office suite into a workspace. Every Nextcloud app on the install can read and write the same registers, and every register surfaces what lives in those apps. The integration runs on three levels at once: sidebar tabs that show linked records, dashboard widgets on every user's home screen, and a shared service layer that lets one app call another's data without anyone copy-pasting between tabs.

Files, mail, and shares stay with the record.

Every object in a register gets an auto-created folder. Drop files into it, write notes against the record, tag rows from the same workspace taxonomy your files use. Add a mail to a project and your colleagues see it without anyone forwarding. When someone shares one of those files in Nextcloud, the share is visible on the record. The case file is part of the workspace, not a parallel storage to keep in sync.

See the integration guide
Your CRM uses your real contacts and calendar.

PipelinQ leans on the Nextcloud contacts and calendar your team already maintains. Adding a deal does not duplicate the customer record, and booking a meeting goes straight into the salesperson's own calendar. No vendor lock-in on the people graph.

Project knowledge, attached to the project.

The wiki, decisions, and meetings for a project all hang off the same register row. xWiki pages, DeciDesk decisions, and meeting notes show up in the right-hand sidebar of every record so the team reads the project from one place.

Open a ticket or lead straight from a Talk message.

Right-click a message in Nextcloud Talk and turn it into a PipelinQ lead, a Procest case, or a DocuDesk request. The Talk conversation stays linked to the new record so the context is one click away.

Outside systems, in the same fabric.

OpenConnector bridges to legacy systems, REST APIs, SOAP endpoints, file drops, and message queues. The remote data flows into the same registers, the same folders, the same sidebar. Users do not learn a second interface to reach the third-party world.

React / MDX usage

Mount in MDX as <IntegrationsBlock/>

Explainer section that pairs a left-rail FAQ with a right-rail product mock. Used on app detail pages and the OpenRegister marketing surfaces to show how the apps fit into a single Nextcloud workspace. Spans the full content column; the eyebrow + h2 + lede sit above the split.

Import & example

import {IntegrationsBlock, IntegrationsItem, MockScene} from '@conduction/docusaurus-preset/components';

<IntegrationsBlock
  eyebrow="Integrations"
  title="Apps that truly work together."
  lede="OpenRegister is what turns an office suite into a workspace..."
  mock={<MockScene preset="openregister-cross-app" />}
>
  <IntegrationsItem
    title="Files, mail, and shares stay with the record."
    cta={{label: "See the integration guide", to: "/docs/openregister/integrations"}}
    defaultOpen
  >
    Every object in a register gets an auto-created folder...
  </IntegrationsItem>
  <IntegrationsItem title="Your CRM uses your real contacts and calendar.">
    PipelinQ leans on the Nextcloud contacts and calendar...
  </IntegrationsItem>
</IntegrationsBlock>

Props

  • eyebrow short uppercase label rendered in KNVB orange with a hex bullet
  • title h2 headline (sentence case, period at the end)
  • lede 1–3-sentence paragraph that frames the integration story
  • mock a node rendered into the right rail (typically <MockScene/> or <AppMock/>)
  • children one or more <IntegrationsItem/> nodes for the left-rail accordion

IntegrationsItem takes title, cta (optional {label, to} button shown inside the open body), defaultOpen (boolean), and children (the answer body, MDX inline markup allowed).
